00:00.quote: It was midnight, and the city of Beijing lay sleeping, wrapped in darkness and dreaming of the hours to come.
00:00.author: Cao Xueqin

01:00.quote: At one o'clock in the morning the lamp was burning low, and the scholar bent over his manuscripts still.
01:00.book: Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio
01:00.author: Pu Songling

02:00.quote: Two in the morning and the moon had crossed half the sky, silent witness to the scholar's sleepless night.
02:00.author: Wu Jingzi

03:00.quote: At three o'clock the watchman's drum echoed through the hutongs, marking the third watch of the night.
03:00.author: Shi Nai'an

04:00.quote: Four in the morning, and already the early risers had begun to kindle their fires and open their stalls.
04:00.author: Lanling Xiaoxiaosheng

05:00.quote: At five o'clock the cock crowed and the horizon began to blush pink over the eastern rooftops.
05:00.author: Cao Xueqin

06:00.quote: Six o'clock, and the morning market had sprung to life with the calls of vendors and the rumble of carts.
06:00.author: Lao She

07:00.quote: Seven in the morning and the children ran through the alley, their laughter ringing like small bells.
07:00.author: Ba Jin

08:00.quote: At eight o'clock the offices opened and a thousand clerks bent their heads over a thousand ledgers.
08:00.author: Mao Dun

09:00.quote: Nine o'clock, and the teahouse was already full, steam rising from a hundred cups of longjing.
09:00.author: Lao She

10:00.quote: At ten o'clock the sun stood well above the rooftops and cast its full light upon the courtyard.
10:00.author: Bing Xin

11:00.quote: Eleven o'clock, and the examination hall fell silent save for the scratch of brushes on paper.
11:00.author: Wu Jingzi

12:00.quote: At noon the sun struck the glazed tiles of the Forbidden City and they blazed like golden fire.
12:00.author: Pu Yi

13:00.quote: One in the afternoon and the city drowsed, shutters half-closed against the summer heat.
13:00.author: Shen Congwen

14:00.quote: Two o'clock, and the river traffic moved slowly, boats laden with silk gliding past the ancient waterfront.
14:00.author: Shen Congwen

15:00.quote: At three in the afternoon the shadows began their slow turn eastward and the air smelled of rain.
15:00.author: Mo Yan

16:00.quote: Four o'clock, the hour when schoolchildren spilled out of their classrooms into the waiting streets.
16:00.author: Ba Jin

17:00.quote: At five o'clock the factories released their workers and the streets swelled with a tide of bicycle bells.
17:00.author: Mao Dun

18:00.quote: Six in the evening, and the aroma of evening rice drifted from every window and doorway in the lane.
18:00.author: Lao She

19:00.quote: Seven o'clock, the lanterns were lit, and the story-teller's audience gathered in the square.
19:00.book: Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio
19:00.author: Pu Songling

20:00.quote: At eight o'clock the household gathered for the evening meal, the candles reflecting their faces in warm gold.
20:00.author: Cao Xueqin

21:00.quote: Nine o'clock, and the children had been put to bed; the adults sat on the steps in the cooling air.
21:00.author: Qian Zhongshu

22:00.quote: At ten o'clock the last lights of the city winked out one by one, leaving the stars unchallenged.
22:00.author: Qian Zhongshu

23:00.quote: Eleven o'clock at night, and the poet still sat at his window, watching the moon rise over the rooftops.
23:00.author: Du Fu

23:59.quote: A minute before midnight, and all the day's joys and sorrows condensed into a single held breath.
23:59.author: Cao Xueqin
